State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 897 · SACRAMENTO, 95814 · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95314

April 14, 1976

76-04-224

Mr. Tom B. Pearson
Post Office Box 1195
Del Mar, California 92014

Dear Mr. Pearson:

I have been asked to respond to your letter of March 26,
1976, with attached correspondence, which, together, recite
the following facts:

(a) In March 1974, you contracted with the Herbert B.
Turner Corporation to construct a duplex in the
City of Del Mar [your letter of 3/25/76 to Assemblyman Craven];

(b) To pay for construction, you received several
loans from the Corporation, and duly executed
several notes on the loans [your letter of
3/25/76 to Assemblyman Craven];

(c) For a period of some months, you made no payments,
whether principal or interest, on one of the notes
[Hedgecock's letter of 2/27/76 to Hamilton];

(d) Eventually, you ended up with two notes secured
by Second Trust Deeds on the condominiums of
the duplex [your letter of 3/25/76 to Assemblyman Craven];

(e) These final secured notes reflected an increased
amount due as a result of the unpaid interest
[Hedgecock's letter of 2/27/76 to Hamilton].

Based on these facts, you ask whether or not "the delayed
payment on the note, at least during the period of the delay,
would have to be reported as a gift."

Mr. Tom B. Pearson
April 14, 1976
Page Two


Neither your letter nor attached correspondence provides
data for the loan and note transactions you describe. With-
out dates, the applicability of the Political Reform Act of
1974 (the Act) is in question. If these transactions occurred
prior to January 7, 1975, the Act (with the exception of
Chapter 8, Ballot Pamphlet) does not apply, and we offer no
opinion as to your reporting requirements under the applicable
provisions of any other laws of the State. If these transac-
tions occurred on or after January 7, 1975, the provisions of
the Act do apply and we offer the following comments for your
guidance.

Gift Issue

§82028 of the Government Code, in pertinent part, defines
a gift to mean:

     ..... any payment to the extent that consid-
     eration of equal or greater value is not
     received. Any person, other than a defend-
     ant in a criminal action, who claims that
     a payment is not a gift by reason of receipt
     of consideration has the burden of proving
     that the consideration received is of equal
     or greater value .....

     82044. "Payment" means a payment, distri-
bution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit,
gift or other rendering of money, property,
services or anything else of value, whether
tangible or intangible.

If, in exchange for the right to make delayed payments on
the note, you did not provide consideration of equal or greater
value, you received a "gift" within the meaning of §82028 of
the Government Code. No answer to the issue of consideration
is apparent from your correspondence. Your letter of 3/26/76
to the Fair Political Practices Commission states:

     I have not reported any such "gifts" because
     the extension of due dates was simply a part
     of the evolutionary process of the construc-
     tion project and did not involve any monetary
     consideration of any kind.

Mr. Tom B. Pearson
April 14, 1976
Page Three

Does this mean that Hedgecock's letter of 2/27/76 to
Hamilton is inaccurate when it states that "trust deeds and
notes which reflect an increased amount due to the unpaid
interest" were executed? If consideration of equal or
greater value was given, there was no gift; if such consideration was not given, there was a gift within the
meaning of Government Code §§82025 and 82044.

Income Issue

Two separate issues involving income also may be involved,
depending upon the facts in your case.

§82030 of the Government Code, in pertinent part, defines
"income" to mean ".... income of any nature from any source,
including but not limited to any ... loan, forgiveness or
payment of indebtedness ...."

If, in exchange for the right to make delayed payments,
you did not provide consideration of equal or greater value,
but simply enjoyed "forgiveness or payment of indebtedness,"
you have received "income" within the meaning of §82030 of
the Government Code, to the extent of the value of the delayed
payment arrangement, provided that this event occurred after
the effective date of the Political Reform Act of 1974. We
offer no opinion as to your reporting requirements under the
applicable provisions of any other laws of the State.

Secondly, the amount of the loans themselves are income
under §82030 of the Government Code. The date of the loan
transaction will determine, however, whether you have any
reporting requirements under the Political Reform Act.
Income, however, need not be reported on a Statement of
Economic Interests until the anniversary date of the public
official's assumption of office. Government Code §87203.
If you have reportable income because of the loan transaction, and subsequent notes were extensions of earlier ones,
or transactions which incorporated earlier obligations, this
should be made clear in your explanation, so that double,
triple, etc., reporting of income does not occur.

I hope that these informal comments will be of some help
to you in solving your question. For your further information,
I enclose a copy of the Political Reform Act of 1974, which
may be of assistance to you in reviewing this matter.

Sincerely,

Edgar A. Kerry
Staff Attorney
Conflicts of Interest
Division

26 March 1976

P.O. Box 1195
Del Mar, Ca. 92014


Fair Political Practices Commission
926 J Street,
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Dear Sirs:

     Enclosed is a copy of a letter to Assemblyman William
A. Jraven which describes a series of events causing me
great concern. Additional supporting documentation is also
enclosed.

     In view of the fact that the Del Mar City Attorney,
Mr. Roger Hedgecock, who is my antagonist in this case,
is a candidate for the office of Supervisor, San Diego
County, and is actively campaigning for that office, it
occurred to me that some of the issues raised by my letter
to Mr. Jraven may also be of interest to your commission.

     Although the broader issues of Conflict of Interest
and Fair Political Practices are of greater significance,
there is one small matter which the Deputy District Attorney
did not address, and which you may be able to resolve.
Mr. Hedgecock,in his letter of February 27, 1976 to Mr.
James Hamilton, asked whether or not "the delayed payments on
the note, at least during the period of the delay, would have
to be reported as a gift." I have not reported any such "gifts"
because the extension of due dates was simply a part of the
evolutionary process of the construction project, and did not
involve any monetary consideration of any kind.

     I would greatly appreciate your review of the matters
raised in my letter to Mr. Jraven, and in particular,
comment on the "gift" question described above.


                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]
                                        Tom B. Pearson

25 March 1976

P.O. Box 1195
Del Mar, Ca. 92014

Assemblyman William A. Craven
550 W. Broadway
Vista, Ca.

Dear Bill,

I am writing to ask your assistance in obtaining an
opinion from the Attorney General on a Conflict of Interest
matter.

In March 1974 I entered into a contract with the
Herbert B. Turner Corporation for the construction of a
duplex in the City of Del Mar. At that time I was serving
as a Councilman of the City of Del Mar. Mr. Turner's
construction foreman was Mr. Jerry Stulce.

During the course of the contract, I signed several
notes to Mr. Turner's company for loans made in the pursuit
of the project. These notes were properly executed and
eventually ended up as two notes secured by two Second Trust
Deeds on the two condominiums of the duplex.

In October, 1975, Mr. Turner terminated the services of
Mr. Stulce, who also held stock in the Herbert B. Turner
Corporation. Mr. Stulce subsequently retained the services
of Mr. Roger Hedgecock, an attorney with the firm of Hizze,
Fletcher and Mack, to seek an accounting of certain business
matters of the Herbert B. Turner Corporation.

It is significant that Mr. Turner, as a builder-developer
in Del Mar, frequently appears before the Del Mar Planning
Commission and the Del Mar City Council on planning and
zoning matters pertaining to his projects, and that Mr.
Hedgecock is the City Attorney for the City of Del Mar who
advises the Planning Commission and City Council on planning
and zoning matters.

QUESTION: By accepting Mr. Stulce as a client, did Mr.
Hedgecock create a situation which could result in his having
to disqualify himself as City Attorney due to potential
conflict of interest?

The enclosed correspondence tells the story of Mr. Hedgecock's
letter to Mr. Turner on behalf of Mr. Stulce seeking certain
information pertaining, among other things, to the construction
of my duplex; and the response by Attorney Arnold Steeleon
behalf of Mr. Turner.

(2)

After receiving Mr. Steele's letter, which was
dated 24 October 1975, Mr. Hedgecock, acting as the City
Attorney, interviewed me on the subject of my financial
disclosures required by Proposition 9, and attempted to
obtain essentially the same information which Mr. Steele
had implicitly declined to provide. Mr. Hedgecock followed
up his interview with a letter on City of Del Mar letterhead
signed by him as City Attorney, dated October 29, 1975,
which reiterated his request for information pertaining to
my business dealings with the Herbert B. Turner Corporation.
The letter clearly threatened an "expansion of the inquiry
into this matter" if I did not comply.

QUESTION: Based on the recitals above, was I, as a City
Councilman, denied access to unbiased counsel from the
City Attorney?

I subsequently retained an attorney, Mr. Eric Lodge,
with the firm of Luce, Forward, Hamilton and Scripps, to
perform an audit of my financial disclosures in accordance
with Proposition 9, and to advise me on a course of action.
A letter to Mr. Hedgecock dated November 6, 1975 resulted
from that advice.

There was no reply from Mr. Hedgecock to my letter of
November 6, 1976. However, I did receive a visit from Mr.
Ken Brown, an investigator for the San Diego District Attorney,
on or about 12 December 1975. Although to date I have not
received a copy of the letter, Mr. Hedgecock had requested
the District Attorney to investigate the completeness of
my compliance with Proposition 9. At the end of the fifteen
or twenty minute interview, Mr. Brown concluded that my
disclosures were in order.

On December 17, 1975 I corresponded with Mr. Hedgecock,
advising him of the investigator's verbal conclusion, and
requesting that Mr. Hedgecock file a confirming letter with
the City Clerk. I have never received an answer to this letter.

On February 27, 1975 Mr. Hedgecock addressed another
letter to the Office of the District Attorney. I learned
about this letter when I called Mr. Brown to find out why
there had been no written conclusion to the investigation.
I was able to obtain a copy from the Del Mar City Clerk.
Mr. Hedgecock acknowledges that Mr. Brown's findings have
concluded that my "filings are legally sufficient." However,
he seeks to perpetuate the investigation, in my opinion,
by raising the question of whether the extension of the
due date of a note constitutes a gift. Mr. Hamilton of the
District Attorney's Office chose not to address that question
in his written response.

QUESTION: What laws are there to protect an elected official
from harassment by a city attorney?

(3)

Upon receiving a bill from Luce, Forward, Hamilton
and Scripps for legal services relating to this Proposition
9 investigation, I filed a claim with the City of Del Mar
for reimbursement. At the hearing on March 15, 1976, at which
point I was no longer a councilman due to the expiration of
my term of office, the City Council denied my claim. The
City Attorney, Mr. Hedgecock, advised the Council that it had
done everything in my power to withhold information, a fact
which is certainly not borne out by the evidence.

QUESTION: Is there a precedent for a City Councilman hiring
a private attorney at government expense when he feels that
the duly appointed City Attorney cannot give him unbiased
advice?

Frankly, Bill, I feel rather abused as a result of
this whole business. I did not approve the hiring of
Roger Hedgecock in the first place, and I have found his
legal advice to be superficial. I have not tried to hide
my criticism where it was warranted. However, if the freedom
of speech, particularly of an elected official in a public
forum, is to be protected, a City Councilman must not be
afraid of reprisal through malicious harassment and
investigations.

I think it is also significant that Roger Hedgecock
is a candidate for the Office of Supervisor in San Diego
County. I have been opposed to his continuation as City
Attorney while he campaigns for public office, for his
pronouncements as an appointed City Attorney cannot help
but be colored by his personal political ambition during the
period of an active campaign.

I am aware that S.B. 1401 attempts to resolve the
obvious conflict where an appointed City Attorney must
advise his clients how to fill out the disclosure forms
and then must prosecute them if they do it wrong. I hope
that bill is successful. However, there is a further need,
at least in my case, for a definition of legal position
and ethics where an appointed City Attorney, who also accepts
outside clients due to the part-time nature of his City
position, can create a situation which jeopardizes the
client-attorney relationship between a City Councilman
and the City Attorney.

Thank you very much for anything you can do to shed
some light on the above matters.

Very sincerely,

Tom B. Pearson

October 21, 1975

Herb Turner Corporation
606 Zuni Drive
Del Mar California 92014

Dear Mr. Turner:

     I have been retained by Jerry O. Stulce,
Secretary-Treasurer of the Herb Turner Corporation,
for the purpose of seeking an accounting as outlined
below:

     It is my understanding that the Herb Turner
Corporation built the Pearson residence on which Mr. Tom
Pearson still owes the corporation a large amount. I
further understand that Mr. Stulce has asked you on
several occasions for an accounting of this debt owed
and you have not furnished any such accounting. Mr.
Stulce indicates that a search of the corporate files
has failed to come up with the Pearson file and therefore
he has been unable to ascertain the status of this account.

     This letter will constitute an official demand
on you on behalf of Mr. Stulce to make a complete accounting
of the Pearson account.

     Mr. Stulce further indicated that he had approved
an agreement in which the Turner Corporation would purchase
20% of a building located at 13th and Cantina in Del Mar for
$20,000 consideration to be part cash and part services
rendered. Recently Mr. Stulce found out that Mr. Philip
Jefferson has indicated to you that he owns the building
at 13th and Cantina and not Mr. James Watkins from whom
you purchased the 20% on behalf of the corporation. Since
work on this project has begun and corporation funds
expended, Mr. Jefferson's indication of ownership comes
as a complete surprise to Mr. Stulce.

Mr. Herbert Turner                    -2-            October 21, 1975


     Consequently, please consider this letter an official
demand on you on behalf of Mr. Sulce to produce any
and all documents signed by you on behalf of the Herbert
Turner Corporation with regard to the property at 13th
and Camino, and, further, to produce a full accounting of
corporation monies expended on this account.

     Despite Mr. Sulce's repeated requests, you
have failed to provide a satisfactory annual accounting
of the corporation. Please consider this letter an
official demand on you on behalf of Mr. Sulce for a
full accounting of the Herbert Turner Corporation for its
immediate past reporting period as well as the current
reporting period.

     Mr. Sulce further indicates that the purpose
of the Herbert Turner Corporation as stated in the Articles
of Incorporation include investment in and improvement of
real property and, further, that you have agreed to apply
your particular expertise in the negotiation and purchase
of property on behalf of the Herbert Turner Corporation.
For the purpose of determining whether or not you have
faithfully performed your duties as a director and officer
of the Herbert Turner Corporation, please list in your
reply to this letter all properties purchased by you
personally or acting with or through other individuals
or corporations including but not limited to United
Telecommunications,Inc.

     Your prompt attention to the requests contained
in this letter would be greatly appreciated. Mr. Sulce
has authorized me to commence legal action on these matters
if a satisfactory reply is not received by me at the above
address by 5:00 p.m. Monday, November 3, 1975.

     Your cooperation in these matters would be
greatly appreciated.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        ROGER HEDGECOCK

RE: HERBERT B. TURNER CORPORATION - STULCE MATTER

Dear Mr. Hedgecock:

My client, Herbert B. Turner Corporation, is notifying Jerry Stulce
as follows:

1)      That he is fired as an employee of Herbert B. Turner
Corporation as of this date.

2)      That he is to deliver the following property of the Herbert
B. Turner Corporation to 606 Zuni Drive, Del Mar, California by
October 29th, 1975:

        (a) Courier Pick-Up Truck
        (b) Lodge Automobile
        (c) Trencher-Back-Hoe Tractor Machine
        (d) All credit cards, including Master Charge

Does Jerry Stulce desire to remain as a Director of Herbert B. Turner
Corporation and of United Telecommunications, Inc.? If not, his resignation should be submitted in writing.

The Annual Financial Statements of the Corporation are being prepared
for the year ending September 30, 1975. As a Shareholder, Mr. Stulce
is entitled to a copy thereof, which he will receive.

As you know, the Herbert B. Turner Corporation has two matters before
the City of Del Mar Council at this time. Your position is vitally attorney of Del Mar, and your representation of Jerry Stulce poses serious possible "conflict of interest" problems. Therefore, I must delve into more details about the properties referred to in your letter.

Be assured that Mr. Stulce will receive the information that he is
entitled to when the Annual Report has been completed. The report
should be forthcoming within a few weeks. Please ask that Mr. Stulce
promptly returns all Corporation Property, and I will do what I can
to get you a copy of the Annual Financial Statements just as soon as
possible.

Yours very truly,

ARNOLD O. STEELE

October 29, 1975

The Honorable Thomas Pearson
Councilman, City of Del Mar
P. O. Box 1195
Del Mar, California 92014

RE: Proposition Filing Requirements (Prop. 9)

Dear Councilman Pearson:

As we discussed last evening at the City Council
meeting (October 27th), I have received certain information
regarding the loan you received from the Herbert B. Turner
Corporation which, if true, would render your reports you have
made under Proposition 9 and the Waxman-Dymally Act incomplete.

In the interest of full public disclosure and to
avoid any expansion of the inquiry into this matter, you have
agreed to furnish me with a copy of the note executed by you
and Mr. Turner and also a copy of the subsequent trust deed
and note which secured this personal loan.

You have also indicated that you have made payments
on this loan on a regular basis. Please furnish me with copies
of some proof of these payments made.

I realize these requests are time consuming and are
an irritating intrusion into your private affairs, but given
the strict enforcement of Proposition 9 by local and state
law enforcement officials, it is necessary, in my opinion, to
ensure a complete public disclosure of these matters.

Thanks for your kind cooperation. Should you have
any questions, please don't hesitate to call me.

Very truly yours,

ROGER HEDGECOCK
City Attorney

November 6, 1975

Roger Hedgecock, Esq.
City Attorney
City of Del Mar
201 15th Street
Del Mar, California 92014

Re: Proposition 9 Filing Requirements

Dear Mr. Hedgecock:

This will respond to your letter of October 29, 1975.

In light of our conversation of October 27 and your letter, I have reviewed the disclosure statement (Form 720) dated May 12, 1975 which I previously filed. I am satisfied that, with respect to financial trans­actions between myself and the Herbert Turner Construction Company, the statement is accurate. If, however, you have received information that would lead you to the conclusion that disclosure statements filed by me are not accurate, I would appreciate receiving such information so that I may consider it in deciding whether an amended statement should be filed. I am, of course, very concerned that all disclosure statements filed by me are in full compliance with the spirit as well as the letter of our state and local disclosure laws. I have always endeavored to comply fully with those requirements and look to you, as City Attorney, for advice in that regard.

As far as supplying copies of the documents in question is concerned, I have not received from you any explanation of why copies of those docu­ments would be required to assure compliance with the law. As I read the forms provided by the state, they require only disclosure of the existence of certain financial relationships and not production of the documents themselves. Therefore, I shall respectfully decline to provide you with copies of those documents until you explain to me 1) the nature of the infor­mation you have that would lead you to believe that the reports I have filed are "incomplete," and 2) how providing copies of those documents or records of loan payments would tend to make my disclosure statements more "complete."

Mr. Roger Hedgecock, City Attorney - 2                    November 6, 1975

     It has come to my attention that you are representing a former
business associate of Herbert Turner with respect to a dispute between
the two of them. It is my understanding that my business relationship
with Mr. Turner or his corporation may, at least in part, be involved in
that dispute. Thus, a further reason for my being reluctant to comply
with the requests contained in your letter of October 29, 1975 is that
it might leave you open to the accusation, however unjust, that you were
using your position as the Del Mar City Attorney to obtain information that
would be useful to you in this dispute between Mr. Turner and his former
employee.

     Please feel free to contact me if you feel that this matter merits
further review since, as I mentioned earlier in this letter, I am relying
on you as our City Attorney to provide me with the advice necessary to
assure that I am complying with the state and local financial disclosure
and conflict of interest requirements.

                                        Sincerely yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Tom B. Pearson
                                        City Councilman

MAR 8 1976

Dear Jim:

Some months ago I referred to your office questions which
had been raised with the sufficiency of Del Mar Councilman Tom Pearson's
disclosure filings.

Since then, Mr. Ken Brown of your office has conducted an
investigation which, as I understand it, demonstrates that for a
period of some months Mr. Pearson made no payments, whether principal
or interest, on a note held by Herbert B. Turner, a contractor who is
often before the Del Mar City Council with projects.

The money loaned by Mr. Turner to Mr. Pearson was used in
the construction of a condominium duplex in Del Mar for Mr. Pearson.

These facts raised the question in my mind of whether or not
such delayed payments must be reported as a gift or whether or not
such delayed payments are not a gift in light of subsequent execution
of trust deeds and notes which reflect an increased amount due to the
unpaid interest.

I understand from Mr. Brown that his preliminary findings
are that Mr. Pearson's filings are legally sufficient. I would like
these findings, however, in writing so that the record in Del Mar is
clear and, further, would request a specific answer to the question of
whether or not the delayed payments on the note, at least during the
period of the delay, would have to be reported as a gift.

Thanks very much for your consideration of this request.

Very truly yours,

sections.conclusion: If transactions occurred prior to January 7, 1975, the Act does not apply. If on or after January 7, 1975: (1) If consideration of equal or greater value was not provided for delayed payment rights, a gift exists under §82028; (2) Forgiveness or payment of indebtedness may constitute income under §82030 if no equal consideration was given; (3) Loan amounts themselves are income under §82030, with reporting dependent on transaction date; (4) Income need not be reported until the anniversary date of assumption of office under §87203; (5) If subsequent notes extended earlier obligations, this should be clarified to avoid duplicate reporting.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester asks whether delayed payments on construction loan notes constitute reportable gifts under the Political Reform Act of 1974, and what income reporting obligations apply to loan transactions and forgiveness of indebtedness.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that if the transactions occurred after January 7, 1975, delayed payment arrangements without equal consideration constitute gifts under §82028, and the forgiveness or extension of indebtedness may constitute reportable income under §82030, though reporting timing depends on the specific transaction dates and whether income reporting is due at the anniversary date of office assumption.

Dear Mr. Hedgecock: 
This will respond to your letter of October 29, 1975. 
In light of our convtrsation of October 27 and your letter, I have 
reviewed the disclosure statement (Form 720) dated Nay 12, 1975 which I 
previously filed. 
I am satisfied that, with respect to financial trans-
acti ons behleen myself and the Herbert Turner Construct; on Company, the 
statement is accurate. If, however, you have received information that 
would lead you to the conclusion that disclosure statements filed by me 
are not accurate, I would appreciate receiving such information so that 
I may consider it in deciding whether an amended statement should be 
filed. 
I am, of course, very concerned that all disclosure statements 
filed by me are in full compliance with the spirit as well as the letter 
of our state and local disclosure laws. 
I have always endeavored to comply 
fully with those requirements and look to you, as City Attorney, for advice 
in that regard. 
As far as supplying copies of the documents in question is concerned, 
I have not received from you any explanation of why copies of those docu-
ments would be required to assure compliance with the law. 
As I read the 
forms provided by the state, they require only disclosure of the existence 
of certain financial relationships and not production of the documents 
themselves. 
Therefore, I shall respectfully decline to provide you with 
copies of those documents until you explain to me 1) the nature of the infor-
mation you have that would lead you to believe that the reports I have filed 
are "incomplete," and 2) how providing copies of those documents or records 
of loan payments \'lOuld tend to make my disclosure statements more "complete."

CONCLUSION: If transactions occurred prior to January 7, 1975, the Act does not apply. If on or after January 7, 1975: (1) If consideration of equal or greater value was not provided for delayed payment rights, a gift exists under §82028; (2) Forgiveness or payment of indebtedness may constitute income under §82030 if no equal consideration was given; (3) Loan amounts themselves are income under §82030, with reporting dependent on transaction date; (4) Income need not be reported until the anniversary date of assumption of office under §87203; (5) If subsequent notes extended earlier obligations, this should be clarified to avoid duplicate reporting.
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 897 · SACRAMENTO, 95814 · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95314 April 14, 1976 76-04-224 Mr. Tom B. Pearson Post Office Box 1195 Del Mar, California 92014 Dear Mr. Pearson: I have been asked to respond to your letter of March 26, 1976, with attached correspondence, which, together, recite the following facts: (a) In March 1974, you contracted with the Herbert B. Turner Corporation to construct a duplex in the City of Del Mar [your letter of 3/25/76 to Assemblyman Craven]; (b) To pay for construction, you received several loans from the Corporation, and duly executed several notes on the loans [your letter of 3/25/76 to Assemblyman Craven]; (c) For a period of some months, you made no payments, whether principal or interest, on one of the notes [Hedgecock's letter of 2/27/76 to Hamilton]; (d) Eventually, you ended up with two notes secured by Second Trust Deeds on the condominiums of the duplex [your letter of 3/25/76 to Assemblyman Craven]; (e) These final secured notes reflected an increased amount due as a result of the unpaid interest [Hedgecock's letter of 2/27/76 to Hamilton]. Based on these facts, you ask whether or not "the delayed payment on the note, at least during the period of the delay, would have to be reported as a gift." Mr. Tom B. Pearson April 14, 1976 Page Two Neither your letter nor attached correspondence provides data for the loan and note transactions you describe. With- out dates, the applicability of the Political Reform Act of 1974 (the Act) is in question. If these transactions occurred prior to January 7, 1975, the Act (with the exception of Chapter 8, Ballot Pamphlet) does not apply, and we offer no opinion as to your reporting requirements under the applicable provisions of any other laws of the State. If these transac- tions occurred on or after January 7, 1975, the provisions of the Act do apply and we offer the following comments for your guidance. Gift Issue §82028 of the Government Code, in pertinent part, defines a gift to mean: ..... any payment to the extent that consid- eration of equal or greater value is not received. Any person, other than a defend- ant in a criminal action, who claims that a payment is not a gift by reason of receipt of consideration has the burden of proving that the consideration received is of equal or greater value ..... 82044. "Payment" means a payment, distri- bution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other rendering of money, property, services or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible. If, in exchange for the right to make delayed payments on the note, you did not provide consideration of equal or greater value, you received a "gift" within the meaning of §82028 of the Government Code. No answer to the issue of consideration is apparent from your correspondence. Your letter of 3/26/76 to the Fair Political Practices Commission states: I have not reported
embedding.summary: This is an FPPC staff attorney response to Tom Pearson regarding whether delayed payments on construction loan notes constitute reportable gifts and income under the Political Reform Act of 1974. The letter provides guidance on the applicability of gift and income reporting requirements based on whether transactions occurred before or after the Act's effective date of January 7, 1975.
